blocks.content: She was in the position she'd been in when he left — on her side, the covers pulled up, her hair against the pillow. She slept deeply sometimes. She always had.
He set the coffee cup on the nightstand. He put his hand on her shoulder.
The stillness beneath his hand was not the stillness of sleep.

He knew the difference. He had been listening to her sleep for fifteen years. He knew every quality of it — the light surface sleep of early morning, the deep rebuilding sleep of a body doing its work. He knew the rhythm of her breathing the way he knew his own hands.
The room was not making that sound.
He said her name once more, very quietly.
